title: scenario_spear_phishing
description: A personalised spear-phishing attack targets a specific employee using harvested social media data.

timeline.title: Targeted Email Arrives
timeline.description: A convincing email referencing the employee's name, team, and current project arrives from a spoofed colleague address.

timeline.title: Malicious Attachment Opened
timeline.description: The employee opens an attached document that silently installs a remote access trojan.

timeline.title: Backdoor Established
timeline.description: Attacker gains persistent access to the employee's machine and begins exfiltrating files.

timeline.title: C2 Traffic Detected
timeline.description: Network monitoring flags unusual outbound traffic to an unfamiliar external IP address.

timeline.title: Endpoint Isolated
timeline.description: The compromised machine is disconnected from the network to halt data exfiltration.

timeline.title: Forensic Investigation
timeline.description: Forensics determines what data was exfiltrated and how the attacker gathered personal details.

node-1.title: Suspicious Personalised Email
node-1.situation: An employee receives an email that appears to be from their direct manager, referencing a real project by name and asking them to open an attached budget spreadsheet urgently. The sender address looks legitimate at a glance but the domain has one character transposed.
node-1.question: What should the employee do first?

options.label: A
options.text: Verify the request by calling or messaging the manager through a trusted channel before opening the attachment.

options.label: B
options.text: Open the attachment immediately — the email content is too specific to be fake.

options.label: C
options.text: Reply to the email asking for confirmation before opening the attachment.

node-2.title: Spoofed Domain Confirmed
node-2.situation: The manager confirms they did not send any email. The IT security team is now aware and wants to determine how the attacker obtained such specific personal details.
node-2.question: What is the most likely source the attacker used to craft the email?

options.label: A
options.text: Publicly available social media profiles and LinkedIn data exposing the employee's team, role, and current projects.

options.label: B
options.text: A previous data breach of the company's customer database.

node-3.title: OSINT Exposure Review
node-3.situation: A quick review confirms that the employee's LinkedIn profile publicly displays their team name, manager, and current project. The attacker used this open-source intelligence (OSINT) to craft a convincing lure.
node-3.question: What preventive measure should be implemented?

options.label: A
options.text: Advise staff to restrict public visibility of work-related details on social media, and deliver OSINT-awareness training.

options.label: B
options.text: Ban all staff from using LinkedIn or any professional social network.

node-4.title: Email Domain Spoofing Control
node-4.situation: IT discovers the organisation lacks DMARC enforcement, which allowed the attacker to spoof the email domain convincingly.
node-4.question: What email security configuration should be applied?

options.label: A
options.text: Configure SPF, DKIM, and DMARC with a reject policy to prevent domain spoofing of outbound and inbound email.

options.label: B
options.text: Add a banner to all inbound emails labelling them as external — no further action is needed.

node-5.title: Scenario Complete
node-5.situation: The spear-phishing attempt was identified before any payload was executed. DMARC enforcement has been applied, staff OSINT awareness has been improved, and the email domain spoofing vector has been closed.
node-5.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
